title: I Really Wanted Sony’s New Mini RGB LED Television to Blow My Mind With Vivid Colors
gist: Wired rates Sony's new Bravia 7 Mark II mini RGB LED TV 6/10, arguing that despite 'True RGB' marketing its picture quality, contrast, and value trail competing mini/micro RGB models from Hisense, TCL, Samsung, and LG as the new display category launches.

insights.claim: Mini and micro RGB LED televisions are reaching market in 2026, with Sony's Bravia 7 Mark II among the first models alongside the Hisense UR9 and TCL RM9L.
insights.speaker: John Brandon
insights.so_what: A new premium display category is opening; watch which panel makers (Sony, Hisense, TCL, Samsung, LG) capture early mindshare and margin as the segment forms.
insights.quote: The age of mini and micro RGB LED televisions is here, and the Sony Bravia 7 Mark II is one of the first models to hit the market, joining the Hisense UR9 and the TCL RM9L.
insights.locator: The age of mini and micro RGB

insights.claim: Mini and micro RGB TVs use tiny red, green, and blue LEDs to emit color through an LCD panel, with micro RGB theoretically more advanced via smaller LEDs enabling pixel-level control.
insights.speaker: John Brandon
insights.so_what: Understanding the mini-vs-micro distinction clarifies where the technical differentiation (and pricing tiers) will sit as the category matures.
insights.quote: Micro RGB is (in theory) more advanced than mini because the LEDs are smaller, allowing for more pixel-level control.
insights.locator: Mini and micro RGB TVs use tiny

insights.claim: Marketing terminology is fragmenting the category, with Sony branding its tech 'True RGB' while Samsung and LG use 'micro RGB,' and Sony reps disputing rivals' claim that smaller LEDs are actually smaller.
insights.speaker: John Brandon
insights.so_what: Confusing, unstandardized branding early in a category can slow consumer adoption and gives reviewers pricing power over which spec claims stick.
insights.quote: Sony reps actually dispute that claim, telling WIRED the LEDs are the same size.
insights.locator: Micro RGB is (in theory) more advanced

insights.claim: The Sony Bravia 7 Mark II is positioned as midrange at $2,300 for the 65-inch (with a $300 discount) versus $3,600 for the flagship Bravia 9 Mark II, yet both share identical underlying mini RGB tech.
insights.speaker: John Brandon
insights.so_what: The $1,300 flagship premium buys only sound and black levels, not core panel tech—evidence Sony is segmenting via features rather than displays.
insights.quote: The Bravia 7 Mark II costs $2,300 for the 65-inch model (with a $300 discount running now), while the Bravia 9 II costs $3,600 at the same size.
insights.locator: The Sony Bravia 7 Mark II is positioned as a midrange

insights.claim: The Bravia 7 Mark II offers the broadest size range of any mini RGB model, from 50 inches up to 98 inches.
insights.speaker: John Brandon
insights.so_what: Widest SKU spread lets Sony cover more price points in the nascent category than competitors, a distribution edge even where picture quality lags.
insights.quote: You’ll also get your pick among the broadest size range of any mini RGB model, starting at 50 inches and running all the way up to a massive 98 inches.
insights.locator: The Bravia 9 Mark II has more speakers

insights.claim: Wired judges the Bravia 7 Mark II overpriced at $2,300, concluding it lacks a midrange price despite midrange performance versus flagship mini RGB models from Samsung, LG, TCL, and Hisense.
insights.speaker: John Brandon
insights.so_what: A credibility-driven outlet flagging poor value on a new-category launch is a demand headwind; Sony may need deeper discounts to move units.
insights.quote: the 7 Mark II does not have a midrange price at $2,300, making me feel it’s overpriced for what you get.
insights.locator: While RGB tech is the real deal

insights.claim: Sony's picture-quality marketing is characterized as a 'word salad of technical terms' that cannot mask the TV underperforming competing mini and micro RGB models.
insights.speaker: John Brandon
insights.so_what: Reviewer skepticism toward True RGB / XR branding signals the marketing narrative is outrunning the product—a gap that can erode premium pricing.
insights.quote: what is essentially a word salad of technical terms propping up the Bravia 7 Mark II can’t hide the fact that it didn’t fare as well as competing mini and micro RGB models.
insights.locator: However, what is essentially a word salad

insights.claim: The Bravia 7 Mark II lacks a DisplayPort and tops out at 120 Hz for a connected PC, while the Hisense UR9 supports DisplayPort with 180 Hz and a variable 330-Hz refresh rate.
insights.speaker: John Brandon
insights.so_what: Hisense out-specs Sony on high-refresh gaming connectivity, a concrete edge for the PC-gamer segment where Sony is ceding ground.
insights.quote: The UR9 supports 180 Hz and a variable 330-Hz refresh rate for smooth, low-latency gameplay.
insights.locator: When you peek behind the television

insights.claim: In direct testing the Hisense UR9 delivered more vivid, responsive gaming and better built-in speaker surround sound than the Bravia 7 Mark II, though Sony's casting was more reliable and its external-speaker Atmos more immersive.
insights.speaker: John Brandon
insights.so_what: Head-to-head, Hisense wins on gaming and integrated audio—the value dimensions most buyers weigh—pressuring Sony's price premium.
insights.quote: the built-in speakers on the Hisense UR9 are much better for surround sound than the Bravia 7 Mark II’s built-in speakers.

insights.claim: Sony's proprietary picture enhancements including XR Contrast Booster and Reality Creation failed to meaningfully improve contrast, brightness, skin-tone accuracy, or upscaled SD content in testing.
insights.speaker: John Brandon
insights.so_what: Sony's software processing—a key differentiator it charges for—underdelivered, weakening the case that its brand justifies the price gap.
insights.quote: XR Contrast Booster didn’t really make a big difference, for example.
insights.locator: Some of the Bravia 7 Mark II enhancements

insights.claim: The Bravia 7 Mark II runs Google TV and bundles Nvidia G-Sync support plus the Sony Pictures Core app, cited as one of the best ways to watch Imax Enhanced high-resolution movies at home.
insights.speaker: John Brandon
insights.so_what: Sony leans on Google TV and its own Pictures Core content pipeline as ecosystem stickiness, an asset rivals like Hisense and TCL lack.
insights.quote: the Sony Pictures Core app, which is still one of the best ways to watch Imax Enhanced high-resolution movies at home.
insights.locator: The Bravia 9 Mark II has more speakers

insights.claim: Google TV setup could not use the Google Home app QR code, forcing manual Gmail credential entry as a workaround before the process smoothed out.
insights.speaker: John Brandon
insights.so_what: Minor onboarding friction on a marquee platform integration—worth watching if it recurs across Google TV launches as a UX drag.
insights.quote: I wasn’t able to use the Google Home app QR code for initial setup.
